Markov, Higher-Order, and Simplicial Analysis

  • Purpose
  • Passage Times and Markov Stability
    • A Subgroup Comparison
  • Higher-Order Analysis
    • Markov Order With MOGen
    • Higher-Order Network
    • Common Paths
    • Path Anomalies
  • Simplicial Analysis
  • Practical Reading Order

Purpose

This tutorial moves beyond a first look at a transition network. It covers:

  • passage times and Markov stability;
  • Markov order and higher-order pathways;
  • path anomalies;
  • simplicial summaries of network shape.

Passage Times and Markov Stability

The trajectories data set has three engagement states over time.

data(trajectories)
df <- as.data.frame(trajectories)

net <- build_network(df, method = "relative")
round(net$weights, 3)
#>            Active Average Disengaged
#> Active      0.698   0.267      0.035
#> Average     0.204   0.610      0.186
#> Disengaged  0.120   0.397      0.483

passage_time() computes expected time to reach one state from another.

pt <- passage_time(net)
pt
#> Mean First Passage Times (3 states)
#> 
#>            Active Average Disengaged
#> Active        2.7     3.6       10.4
#> Average       5.5     2.3        8.0
#> Disengaged    6.2     2.8        5.4
#> 
#> Stationary distribution:
#>     Active    Average Disengaged 
#>     0.3719     0.4431     0.1850
plot(pt)

The diagonal is the expected return time. Off-diagonal cells are expected first passage times.

markov_stability() summarizes each state.

ms <- markov_stability(net)
ms
#> Markov Stability Analysis
#> 
#>       state persistence stationary_prob return_time sojourn_time
#>      Active      0.6976          0.3719        2.69         3.31
#>     Average      0.6099          0.4431        2.26         2.56
#>  Disengaged      0.4831          0.1850        5.41         1.93
#>  avg_time_to_others avg_time_from_others
#>                6.99                 5.84
#>                6.74                 3.20
#>                4.47                 9.16
plot(ms)

Important quantities include persistence, stationary probability, return time, sojourn time, and accessibility from other states.

A Subgroup Comparison

Filter learners who are active more than half the observed time.

active_count <- rowSums(df == "Active", na.rm = TRUE)
mostly_active <- df[active_count > ncol(df) / 2, ]

net_active <- build_network(mostly_active, method = "relative")
round(net_active$weights, 3)
#>            Active Average Disengaged
#> Active      0.819   0.164      0.017
#> Average     0.683   0.288      0.029
#> Disengaged  0.643   0.143      0.214

passage_time(net_active)
#> Mean First Passage Times (3 states)
#> 
#>            Active Average Disengaged
#> Active        1.3     6.1       52.0
#> Average       1.5     5.4       51.3
#> Disengaged    1.5     6.3       41.7
#> 
#> Stationary distribution:
#>     Active    Average Disengaged 
#>     0.7895     0.1866     0.0240
markov_stability(net_active)
#> Markov Stability Analysis
#> 
#>       state persistence stationary_prob return_time sojourn_time
#>      Active      0.8191          0.7895        1.27         5.53
#>     Average      0.2885          0.1866        5.36         1.41
#>  Disengaged      0.2143          0.0240       41.75         1.27
#>  avg_time_to_others avg_time_from_others
#>               29.06                 1.50
#>               26.38                 6.20
#>                3.91                51.64

This is a descriptive subgroup comparison. For formal edge-level comparison, use permutation tests on grouped networks.

Higher-Order Analysis

Now use the human-AI action sequences. A first-order network asks what follows the current action. Higher-order analysis asks whether earlier context changes the next action.

data(human_long)

set.seed(3)
sessions <- sample(unique(human_long$session_id), 160)
human_sub <- human_long[human_long$session_id %in% sessions, ]

human_net <- build_network(
  human_sub,
  method = "relative",
  action = "code",
  actor = "session_id",
  time = "timestamp"
)

human_net
#> Transition Network (relative probabilities) [directed]
#>   Weights: [0.012, 0.587]  |  mean: 0.111
#> 
#>   Weight matrix:
#>             Command Correct Frustrate Inquire Interrupt Refine Request Specify
#>   Command     0.256   0.085     0.045   0.065     0.036  0.040   0.168   0.257
#>   Correct     0.099   0.089     0.145   0.064     0.064  0.103   0.121   0.266
#>   Frustrate   0.096   0.123     0.186   0.066     0.033  0.159   0.099   0.156
#>   Inquire     0.170   0.148     0.072   0.204     0.097  0.063   0.091   0.104
#>   Interrupt   0.255   0.076     0.090   0.131     0.128  0.048   0.062   0.141
#>   Refine      0.070   0.091     0.074   0.053     0.053  0.107   0.119   0.420
#>   Request     0.098   0.017     0.046   0.101     0.058  0.029   0.029   0.587
#>   Specify     0.259   0.053     0.091   0.072     0.185  0.050   0.061   0.188
#>             Verify
#>   Command    0.048
#>   Correct    0.050
#>   Frustrate  0.081
#>   Inquire    0.050
#>   Interrupt  0.069
#>   Refine     0.012
#>   Request    0.035
#>   Specify    0.041
#>    [ reached 'max' / getOption("max.print") -- omitted 1 row ] 
#> 
#>   Initial probabilities:
#>   Specify       0.678  ████████████████████████████████████████
#>   Command       0.171  ██████████
#>   Request       0.050  ███
#>   Interrupt     0.035  ██
#>   Frustrate     0.020  █
#>   Refine        0.020  █
#>   Inquire       0.015  █
#>   Correct       0.010  █
#>   Verify        0.000

Markov Order With MOGen

build_mogen() fits models at increasing order and reports information criteria.

mg <- build_mogen(human_net, max_order = 3)
summary(mg)
#> Multi-Order Generative Model (MOGen) Summary
#> 
#>   States: Command, Correct, Frustrate, Inquire, Interrupt, Refine, Request, Specify, Verify
#>   Paths: 190 | Observations: 3840
#> 
#>   Optimal order: 3 (by aic)
plot(mg)

Extract order-specific transitions:

head(mogen_transitions(mg, order = 2), 12)

If a higher order is preferred, the next state depends on more than the current state. If order 1 is preferred, local higher-order patterns may still exist.

Higher-Order Network

build_hon() identifies contexts where adding history changes the outgoing transition distribution.

hon <- build_hon(human_net)
hon
#> Higher-Order Network (HON)
#>   Nodes:        553 (9 first-order states)
#>   Edges:        1944
#>   Max order:    5 (requested 5)
#>   Min freq:     1
#>   Trajectories: 190

head(hon$ho_edges[order(-hon$ho_edges$count), ], 12)

Focus on higher-order edges where the context-specific probability differs meaningfully from the first-order pattern.

Common Paths

path_counts() is descriptive. It lists observed paths by frequency.

path_counts(human_net, k = 3, top = 15)

Common paths are not automatically surprising. They may be common because their individual states are common.

Path Anomalies

build_hypa() tests which paths occur more or less often than expected.

hypa <- build_hypa(human_net)
hypa
#> HYPA: Path Anomaly Detection
#>   Order k:      3
#>   Edges:        1778
#>   Anomalous:    22 (alpha=0.05, p_adjust=BH)
#>     Over-repr:  8
#>     Under-repr: 14

head(hypa$over, 10)
head(hypa$under, 10)

Over-represented paths are routines that occur more than the null model expects. Under-represented paths are sequences that are avoided relative to that expectation.

Simplicial Analysis

Simplicial analysis treats groups of connected nodes as higher-dimensional objects. It is a way to describe the shape of the network, not just the list of edges.

sc <- build_simplicial(human_net, threshold = 0.05)
sc
#> Clique Complex 
#>   9 nodes, 511 simplices, dimension 8
#>   Density: 100.0%  |  Mean dim: 3.51  |  Euler: 1
#>   f-vector: (f0=9 f1=36 f2=84 f3=126 f4=126 f5=84 f6=36 f7=9 f8=1)
#>   Betti: b0=1
#>   Nodes: Command, Correct, Frustrate, Inquire, Interrupt, Refine, Request, Specify, Verify

betti_numbers(sc)
#> b0 b1 b2 b3 b4 b5 b6 b7 b8 
#>  1  0  0  0  0  0  0  0  0
euler_characteristic(sc)
#> [1] 1
simplicial_degree(sc)

Read Betti numbers as:

  • beta 0: connected components;
  • beta 1: loops;
  • beta 2: enclosed voids.
plot(sc)

Persistent homology varies the threshold and tracks which topological features persist.

ph <- persistent_homology(human_net, n_steps = 20)
ph
#> Persistent Homology
#>   20 filtration steps [0.5867 → 0.0059]
#>   Features: b0: 9 (1 persistent)  |  b1: 2 (0 persistent)  |  b3: 70 (70 persistent) 
#>   Longest-lived:
#>     b0: 0.5867 → 0.0000 (life: 0.5867)
#>     b0: 0.5867 → 0.1587 (life: 0.4280)
#>     b0: 0.5867 → 0.1587 (life: 0.4280)
plot(ph)

Q-analysis studies connectivity through shared faces.

qa <- q_analysis(sc)
qa
#> Q-Analysis (max q = 8)
#>   Components: q8:1 q7:1 q6:1 q5:1 q4:1 q3:1 q2:1 q1:1 q0:1
#>   Fully connected at all q levels
#>   Structure: Command:8 Correct:8 Frustrate:8 Inquire:8 Interrupt:8 Refine:8 Request:8 Specify:8 Verify:8
plot(qa)

Practical Reading Order

Use the methods in this order:

  1. Estimate and validate a first-order transition network.
  2. Use passage_time() and markov_stability() to describe movement.
  3. Use build_mogen() to check whether higher order is needed globally.
  4. Use build_hon() and path_counts() to inspect local pathways.
  5. Use build_hypa() when the question is statistical surprise.
  6. Use simplicial tools when the question is network shape.
